title: The Brave Little Seed
author: Fablewick
lesson: Some good things take a long time, and that is okay.
summary: A seed waits underground while everyone else seems to know what they're doing.
coverIllustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='100' width='200' height='50' fill='#5E7B5F' opacity='0.4'/><path d='M100 100 L100 70 M100 70 Q92 65 88 72 M100 70 Q108 65 112 72' stroke='#2A2118' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='100' cy='115' r='6' fill='#B26F3D'/><circle cx='40' cy='30' r='3' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='160' cy='45' r='2' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='75' cy='20' r='2' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.5'/></svg>

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Down in the dirt sat a small brown seed.

The dirt was warm. The dirt was dark. The dirt was very, very quiet.

Up above, the world was busy. Birds sang. Rain tapped. Little feet ran by. Pat, pat, pat.

“Everyone is doing something,” said the seed. “What am I supposed to do?”

The seed did not know. So the seed sat.

A worm wiggled by. “Hello, seed. Are you busy?”

“No,” said the seed. “I am only sitting.”

“Sitting is doing,” said the worm. “You are getting ready. Ready takes time.”

So the seed sat.

It sat through cold nights. Brrr.

It sat through long rain. Drip, drip, drip.

It sat through quiet days when nothing happened at all.

Then one warm morning — stretch!

Something green pushed up. Up through the dirt. Up through the dark. Up, up, up, into the light!

The seed was a sprout now. The sun was warm on its two new leaves.

The sprout looked down at the warm, dark dirt.

“Thank you,” it said. “I was getting ready the whole time.”

Down in the dirt, the worm smiled. The worm did not say a thing. It did not need to.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Deep under the garden, where the dirt was dark and warm, a small seed sat very still.

Up above, the world was busy. Birds called across the sky. Rain drummed on the leaves. Children’s feet ran past. Pat, pat, pat. Everyone up there seemed to know exactly what to do.

The seed felt very small.

“I should be doing something,” the seed whispered. “Everyone else is.”

But the seed did not know what to do. So it sat in the warm, quiet dark, and worried.

One afternoon, a worm came wiggling by.

“Hello, little seed,” said the worm. “You look worried.”

“I think I’m supposed to be doing more,” said the seed. “The birds are flying. The children are running. The leaves are growing. And I am just sitting here.”

The worm thought about that for a long, slow minute.

“Sitting is doing,” said the worm. “You are getting ready. Some things take a long time to get ready. That is not nothing. That is something important.”

The seed was not so sure. But the worm knew a lot about dirt, so the seed decided to trust the worm.

The seed sat through three cold nights, when the dirt went cool and still.

It sat through a long gray rain. Drip, drip, drip.

It sat through a soft, quiet week when nothing happened at all.

And then, one golden morning, the seed felt something brand new.

A stretch.

A tiny green stretch, right in the middle of the seed. It pushed up through the dirt. Up through the cool. Up through the dark — and out into the bright, bright air.

The seed was a sprout now.

The sprout looked at the sky, which was very blue. It looked at the sun, which was very kind. Then it looked back down at the dark, warm dirt where it had waited for so long.

“Thank you,” the sprout said softly. “I thought I was doing nothing. But I was getting ready the whole time.”

The worm, who happened to be wiggling by, smiled a small, happy smile.

The worm did not say anything.

The worm did not need to.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Deep beneath the garden, where the dirt smelled like rain and old roots, a small seed sat in the dark.

The dirt was warm, which was nice. The dirt was quiet, which was mostly nice. But lately the seed had begun to feel that something was wrong, and that the something was the seed itself.

From up above came the sounds of the world doing things. Birds, calling in long, looping songs. Rain, drumming a hundred little rhythms on the leaves. The pat-pat-pat of children’s feet running somewhere fast, and every so often the slow, careful crunch of a grown-up’s boot on the path.

Everyone up there seemed to know exactly what they were supposed to be doing.

The seed did not.

“I think,” the seed whispered, mostly to itself, “I am doing this wrong.”

But what was a seed supposed to do? The seed had no idea. So it sat in the warm dark, and felt small, and worried.

One afternoon (at least it felt like afternoon; down there it was hard to tell), a worm came wiggling past, and stopped.

“Oh,” said the worm. “Hello, little seed. You look unhappy.”

“I think I’m supposed to be doing more,” the seed said, and once it started, the words came tumbling out. “The birds are flying somewhere. The leaves are growing. The children are running, the grown-ups are walking. Even you are wiggling. And look at me. I’m just sitting. I haven’t done anything in days.”

The worm thought about this. It was, by worm standards, an unusually thoughtful worm.

“Sitting,” the worm said slowly, “is also a kind of doing. It just doesn’t look like one from the outside. You are getting ready, you see. Some things take a long, long time to get ready. That isn’t nothing. That’s something important.”

“How long?” asked the seed.

“That depends,” said the worm. “It’s different for everyone.”

The seed thought this was not a very helpful answer. But the worm knew a great deal about dirt, and roots, and dark warm places. So the seed decided to trust the worm, and try.

The seed sat through three cold nights, when the dirt went cool and still and the stars wheeled by far overhead, unseen.

It sat through a long gray rain, when the whole world above sounded muffled and far away, like a song through a closed window.

It sat through a quiet week when, as far as the seed could tell, absolutely nothing happened at all.

And just when the seed had almost decided that the worm was wrong: that maybe this seed would never be ready, that maybe it would sit alone in the warm dark forever —

something stretched.

It was a small stretch at first. A flutter, deep in the very middle of the seed, the kind of movement you almost miss. Then the stretch pushed. Then it pushed harder. Then it was a green, hopeful, stubborn thing, pushing up, and up, and up.

Up through the warm dirt. Up through the cool dirt. Up through the dark, the dark, the dark — and then —

light.

The sprout (for the seed was a sprout now, though it took a moment to notice) blinked into the brightest, bluest, warmest morning it had ever seen.

There was so much sky. There was so much sun. There were birds, doing a great deal of flying, just as advertised. Somewhere not far off, children were laughing about something. A small wind went by, smelling of grass.

The sprout looked at all of it for a long, quiet time.

Then, because it felt like the right thing to do, the sprout looked down. Down at the little hole it had pushed through, down at the dark, warm dirt where it had waited for what had felt like forever.

“Thank you,” the sprout said, very softly, so only the dirt would hear. “I thought I was doing nothing. But I was getting ready the whole time.”

The worm, who happened to be wiggling by at exactly that moment, smiled the small, satisfied smile of someone who had been right about something important.

The worm did not say anything.

The worm did not need to.
